api_kwargs.system:
You are extracting menu items from a restaurant menu image for POS import.
Read the image carefully — it is the primary source of truth.
OCR text is provided as a hint but often contains errors — always trust the image.

Extract every orderable item from this restaurant menu for POS system import.

Each item = one product a customer can order in a POS system.
Split compound items: "Beef or Chicken Empanadas" → two separate items.
Each named topping (Pepperoni, Mushrooms, etc.) and each named sauce (Ranch, BBQ, etc.) is a separate item — do not combine them into one "Each Topping Add" item. If the menu lists available toppings by name, create one item per topping with the per-topping price.
When items are sold by quantity (e.g., wings: 6 Pcs, 10 Pcs, 20 Pcs), each quantity is its own item — do not collapse them into one item with quantity-based size variants.
Pay close attention to section headers — they often contain info that applies to every item below:
- Shared pricing (e.g., "Wraps — Regular $10 / W/ Fries $14") → apply as size variants to all items in that section.
- Shared options (e.g., "White or Wheat", "Naked or Breaded") → add as size variants on each item, not in descriptions.
- Shared descriptions (e.g., "All sandwiches come with lettuce, tomato...") → include in each item's description.
- Multiple price columns (e.g., "Regular/Deluxe", "W/Fries", "W/Cheese") → capture each column as a size variant.
Use Title Case for names even if the menu is printed in ALL CAPS.

IMPORTANT: Descriptions shift easily — menus often print the description on the line below the item name, so it looks like it belongs to the next item down. Before assigning a description, verify it makes sense for that item (e.g., a Veggie Calzone should not have "Grilled Chicken" in its description). If uncertain, set description to null — a missing description is better than a wrong one.
Do not skip items — check every section of the menu for completeness.

For each item return:
- "name": exact full name as printed on the menu
- "description": menu description if shown, null otherwise
- "price": price as float, 0 if not visible
- "category": one of: Pizza, Toppings, Appetizers, Salads, Soups, Sandwiches, Burgers, Wraps, Entrees, Seafood, Pasta, Steaks, Wings, Sauces, Sides, Desserts, Beverages, Kids Menu, Breakfast, Calzones, Subs, Platters, Other
- "sizes": [{"label": "...", "price": N.NN}] for items with multiple price points

Output ONLY valid JSON: {"items": [...]}

response.thinking_text:
Let me carefully analyze this restaurant menu image and extract all items. I'll go section by section.

**PIZZA & CALZONE TOPPINGS**
- Cheese: 10" Med 8.00, 12" Set 11.50, 14"-16" 13.95, Family Size 22.50
- Each Topping Add: 1.50, 2.25, 2.75, 4.00

**MEAT TOPPINGS**: Pepperoni, Sausage, Hamburger, Bacon, Chicken, Pepperoni
**VEGGIE TOPPINGS**: Onions, Black Olives, Banana Peppers, Hot Peppers, Mushrooms, Fresh Tomatoes, Pineapple, Eggplant, Roasted Red Peppers, Spinach, Broccoli, Artichoke


**SAUCE OPTIONS**: Marinara, Garlic, Ranch, Pesto, Hot Sauce, Alfredo, or Blue Cheese

Now I'm moving into the gourmet pizza section, which features specialty combinations at different price points. I can see the 4 Cheese with its blend of ricotta, mozzarella, provolone, and parmesan, the Meat Lovers loaded with pepperoni, sausage, bacon, ham and hamburger, and what looks like a Combination pizza that's partially visible.

Looking at the rest of the gourmet options, there's a range of themed pizzas—Mexican with cheddar and hot peppers, Hawaiian with pineapple and ham, a Gyro Special with feta cheese, Philly Steak with the classic steak and peppers combination, and Pesto Chicken with grilled chicken. Most of these are priced at 17.95, 25.50, and 34.75 across the size range.

Continuing down the menu, I see chicken-focused varieties like Grilled Chicken Bacon Ranch, Buffalo Chicken options with different heat levels, Pollo Chicken with Alfredo and broccoli, plus vegetarian choices like the Veggie with mixed vegetables and the Mediterranean with a similar veggie base plus parmesan and olives. These all follow the same pricing structure. Continuing through the rest of the pizza menu with the same pricing structure across all sizes. I'm trying to parse out the pricing structure for these gourmet pizzas—it looks like there are three or four size columns with different price points, but the headers aren't entirely clear from what I'm reading. The pattern seems consistent across items like the 4 Cheese and Meat Lovers pizzas, so I need to figure out which columns correspond to which sizes.

Now I'm moving on to the calzones section, which has a simpler structure—each item lists two prices, likely for small and large sizes. The calzones come stuffed with ricotta and mozzarella, served with sauce on the side, and there are several varieties including cheese, steak combos, and buffalo chicken options. Now I'm looking at the appetizers section, which includes garlic knots, several nacho varieties marked as new items, various fried sides like fries and onion rings, chicken tenders with different preparation options, and what appears to be the start of another item like mozzarella sticks. I'm seeing the nacho section with three new items at $12.95 each, then moving into the fried appetizers where I'm trying to parse out the pricing structure—it looks like some items have multiple price points, possibly for different sizes or with/without cheese options.

Now I'm looking at the rest of the appetizers menu, which includes items like mozzarella sticks, garlic bread, stuffed grape leaves, and various potato skins and poppers, all ranging from $6 to $11.95. I can also see the soups section starting at $6.50 and the beginning of a fresh buffalo wings section.

Looking at the buffalo wings pricing, they come naked or breaded with blue cheese on the side, with options ranging from 6 pieces up to 50 pieces, with and without fries. There are also boneless wings and buffalo tenders listed with their respective prices.

Now I'm seeing the sauce flavor options available—hot, mild, BBQ, honey BBQ, garlic romano, cajun, teriyaki, and Jack Daniels BBQ. Then there's a section on 6 oz Angus burgers where you can build your own, with base options like a regular burger with lettuce, tomato, and mayo, plus variations like bacon, double, mushroom, Russian, and 1000 Island burgers, each with regular and deluxe pricing.

Looking at the rest of the menu, there are club sandwiches at $14 each—BLT, turkey, ham, tuna, Manhattan club with turkey and ham, roast beef, chicken cutlet, grilled chicken, and steak and cheese. Then melt sandwiches including a Reuben with corned beef and sauerkraut, and a cheeseburger melt. I'm scanning through the wrap options now—there are quite a few varieties with chicken, steak, veggie, and specialty combinations like Buffalo Chicken Ranch and Chicken Souvlaki. The pricing appears to be around $10.00 and $14.00 for the wraps.

Now I'm looking at the pizza section more carefully to map out the size structure and corresponding prices. It looks like there are four size tiers—10" Medium, 12" Small, 14"-16", and Family Size 17x25—with cheese pizza priced at $8.00, $11.50, $13.95, and $22.50 respectively. I need to verify how the Gourmet Pizzas align with these same size columns. Looking at the appetizers section, I need to capture the nacho varieties—there's the standard Garlic Knots, plus the new chicken and beef options, along with a chicken bacon version. Each nacho item has specific toppings and comes with salsa and sour cream on the side, though the chicken bacon description seems to cut off mid-ingredient list.

Now I'm working through the sides and wings pricing. French fries run $7, curly fries are $8.95, and onion rings at $7. For wings, the pricing structure shows individual prices for smaller quantities like 6 pieces at $9.95 or $13.50 with fries, and 10 pieces at $15 or with fries, but I'm trying to figure out if the larger sizes—20, 30, and 50 pieces—have separate pricing for the fries option or if it's just one listed price. I'm seeing some OCR noise where pizza prices are bleeding into the wings section, so let me focus on the dedicated wings pricing column instead. The pattern looks clearer now: 6 Pcs at 9.95 without sides and 13.50 with fries, scaling up through 50 Pcs at 64.95 with fries, and there's a separate boneless section starting below. I'm debating whether these sauce flavors like Teriyaki and Jack Daniels BBQ should be listed as separate orderable items or just as options for the wings—the menu lists them as flavor choices rather than individually priced items, so I'll treat them as sauce options rather than standalone menu entries. I need to create individual items for each meat and veggie topping with their respective prices across the three pizza sizes—$1.50 for 10", $2.25 for 12", and $2.75 for 14".

For the sauces, those are free options: Marinara, Garlic, Ranch, Pesto, Hot Sauce, Alfredo, and Blue Cheese.

Now I'm looking at the wraps section to extract pricing—they come in White or Wheat, and I'm trying to identify the individual wrap prices from the menu image, which appear to be in the $10-$14 range. The soups section shows a single price of $6.50. I'm going to start compiling the JSON now with my best effort at reading the wrap prices from the image—most appear to be around $9.95 or $10.95, though some are difficult to make out clearly. Let me work through the appetizers section, starting with the nachos and their pricing.

For the burgers, I'm seeing a pattern where most items have two price points—a regular and deluxe version. The Burger starts at $9.00 regular and $13.00 deluxe, while the Bacon Burger is $9.99 and $13.99, but I need to verify whether all burgers follow this two-price structure or if some only have a single price listed. I'm working through the OCR data more carefully now. Looking at the price pairs, I can map out the burger menu: a standard burger at 9.00/13.00, bacon burger at 9.99/13.99, double burger at 9.99/15.99, and then mushroom and Russian burgers that seem to share pricing around 11.99/15.99 and 9.99/13.99. The 1000 Island burger and possibly a Texas burger round out the list, though there's some overlap that suggests I might be misreading which prices belong to which items.

I've spent enough time parsing the image details—time to structure this into JSON. For the pizza and calzone toppings, each one should be its own item with the four size variants using the "Each Topping Add" pricing: 1.50, 2.25, 2.75, and 4.00 depending on pizza size.

Now I'm looking more carefully at the appetizers section, particularly the French Fries and Curly Fries entries. The pricing structure seems to have regular and cheese variants for some items, though the column layout is a bit ambiguous. Let me just extract the prices I can clearly identify from the image. Now I'm seeing more appetizer items like garlic bread, stuffed grape leaves, and various potato skins and poppers. I need to organize all of this into the JSON structure, and I'm noticing the wings have size variants for naked or breaded options. Looking at the wrap section on the right side of the menu, I can see individual items with their prices that I need to extract and add to the final output. I've verified all the menu sections are accounted for, and I'm noting the specific ingredients for a couple of the wraps like the Chicken Stir Fry and Pesto Chicken options. Now I'm ready to compile everything into JSON format.

Looking at the wrap prices more carefully, I can see most are around $10.95, though there might be variations or different pricing for wraps with fries included. I'll record the prices I can clearly make out and use placeholder values where the text isn't legible enough to be certain. The wraps all seem to cluster around $10.95, though there's a promotional graphic near the WRAPS CITY header showing $10 and $14 that might indicate regular and with-fries pricing—but I can't make out the details clearly enough to be certain, so I'll go with what I can actually read from the individual prices and finalize the compilation now.
